title: हनुमान अष्टक
devanagariText:
॥ श्री हनुमान अष्टक ॥

बाल समय रबि भक्षि लियो तब,
तीनहुँ लोक भयो अँधियारो।
ताहि सों त्रास भयो जग को,
यह संकट काहु सों जात न टारो॥
देवन आनि करी बिनती तब,
छाँड़ि दियो रबि कष्ट निवारो।
को नहिं जानत है जग में कपि,
संकट मोचन नाम तिहारो॥१॥

बालि की त्रास कपीस बसै गिरि,
जात महाप्रभु पंथ निहारो।
चौंकि महामुनि साप दियो तब,
चाहिय कौन बिचार बिचारो॥
कैद्ध महाप्रभु सो दोउ लरै तब,
सुग्रीवहिं मरि कै निहारो।
है प्रभु दीन दयालु कृपालु कपि,
निकसि गयो मारो अहि मारो॥२॥

अंगद के संग लेन गए सिय,
खोज कपीस यह बैन उचारो।
जीवत ना बचिहौ हम सों जु,
बिना सुधि लाये इहाँ पगु धारो॥
हेरि थके तट सिंधु सबे तब,
सीता सुधि कोउ न पारो।
जानि कहाँ तजि प्रान कहे सब,
बीर बजरंग बीर न हारो॥३॥

राम बिरह मकरंद कहँ रे,
मधुकर भानु मास गए कारो।
तापस बंध छुड़ाय सब ऋषिन,
ताहि देखाय सिया महतारो॥
चाहत सीय असोक बाटिका,
गीध सबै जाय के मारो।
सीता की सुधि राम को दीन्हीं,
बजरंगी बिरद सँभारो॥४॥

रावन त्रास दई सिय को सब,
राक्षसिन सों कही सोक निवारो।
ताहि समय हनुमान महाप्रभु,
जाय महा रजनीचर मारो॥
चहुँ दिसि फिरि बज्र की छाया,
तापह ताहि मरो जिम तारो।
दे असिका रघुनाथ पठाए,
बजरंगी बहु बैरिहि मारो॥५॥

बान लग्यो उर लछिमन के तब,
प्रान तजे सुत रावन मारो।
लै गृह बैद्य सुषेन समेत,
तबै गिरि लंका में डारो॥
आनि संजीवन हाथ दई तब,
लछिमन के तुम प्रान उबारो।
को नहिं जानत है जग में कपि,
संकट मोचन नाम तिहारो॥६॥

रावन जुद्ध अजान कियो तब,
नाग की फाँसी में सबको मारो।
श्रीरघुनाथ समेत सबै दल,
मोह भयो यह संकट भारो॥
आनि खगेस तबै हनुमान जू,
बंधन काटि सुत्रास निवारो।
को नहिं जानत है जग में कपि,
संकट मोचन नाम तिहारो॥७॥

बंधु समेत जबै अहिरावन,
लै रघुनाथ पाताल सिधारो।
देबिहिं पूजि भली बिधि सों बलि,
देउ सबै मिलि मंत्र बिचारो॥
जाय सहाय भयो तब ही,
अहिरावन सैन्य समेत संहारो।
को नहिं जानत है जग में कपि,
संकट मोचन नाम तिहारो॥८॥

॥ दोहा ॥
लाल देह लाली लसे, अरु धरि लाल लँगूर।
बज्र देह दानव दलन, जय जय जय कपि सूर॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Hanuman Ashtak ||

Baal Samay Rabi Bhakshi Liyo Tab,
Teenhun Lok Bhayo Andhiyaro.
Taahi Son Traas Bhayo Jag Ko,
Yah Sankat Kaahu Son Jaat Na Taaro.
Devan Aani Kari Binati Tab,
Chhandi Diyo Rabi Kasht Nivaaro.
Ko Nahin Jaanat Hai Jag Mein Kapi,
Sankat Mochan Naam Tihaaro.||1||

Baali Ki Traas Kapees Basai Giri,
Jaat Mahaaprabhu Panth Nihaaro.
Chaunki Mahaamuni Saap Diyo Tab,
Chaahiy Kaun Bichaar Bichaaro.
Kaiddh Mahaaprabhu So Dou Larai Tab,
Sugreevhin Mari Kai Nihaaro.
Hai Prabhu Deen Dayaalu Kripalu Kapi,
Nikasi Gayo Maaro Ahi Maaro.||2||

Angad Ke Sang Len Gaye Siy,
Khoj Kapees Yah Bain Uchaaro.
Jeevat Na Bachihau Ham Son Ju,
Bina Sudhi Laaye Ihan Pagu Dhaaro.
Heri Thake Tat Sindhu Sabe Tab,
Seeta Sudhi Kou Na Paaro.
Jaani Kahan Taji Praan Kahe Sab,
Beer Bajrang Beer Na Haaro.||3||

Raam Birah Makrand Kahan Re,
Madhukar Bhaanu Maas Gaye Kaaro.
Taapas Bandh Chhudaay Sab Rishin,
Taahi Dekhaay Siya Mahtaaro.
Chaahat Seey Ashok Baatika,
Geedh Sabai Jaay Ke Maaro.
Seeta Ki Sudhi Raam Ko Deenhi,
Bajrangi Birad Sambhaaro.||4||

Raavan Traas Dai Siy Ko Sab,
Raakshasin Son Kahi Sok Nivaaro.
Taahi Samay Hanumaan Mahaaprabhu,
Jaay Maha Rajneechir Maaro.
Chahun Disi Phiri Bajra Ki Chhaaya,
Taapah Taahi Maro Jim Taaro.
De Asika Raghunaath Pathaaye,
Bajrangi Bahu Bairihi Maaro.||5||

Baan Lagyo Ur Lachhiman Ke Tab,
Praan Taje Sut Raavan Maaro.
Lai Grih Baidya Sushen Samet,
Tabai Giri Lanka Mein Daaro.
Aani Sanjeevan Haath Dai Tab,
Lachhiman Ke Tum Praan Ubaaro.
Ko Nahin Jaanat Hai Jag Mein Kapi,
Sankat Mochan Naam Tihaaro.||6||

Raavan Juddh Ajaan Kiyo Tab,
Naag Ki Phansi Mein Sabko Maaro.
Shri Raghunaath Samet Sabai Dal,
Moh Bhayo Yah Sankat Bhaaro.
Aani Khages Tabai Hanumaan Ju,
Bandhan Kaati Sutraas Nivaaro.
Ko Nahin Jaanat Hai Jag Mein Kapi,
Sankat Mochan Naam Tihaaro.||7||

Bandhu Samet Jabai Ahiraavan,
Lai Raghunaath Paataal Sidhaaro.
Debihin Pooji Bhali Bidhi Son Bali,
Deu Sabai Mili Mantra Bichaaro.
Jaay Sahaay Bhayo Tab Hi,
Ahiraavan Sainy Samet Sanhaaro.
Ko Nahin Jaanat Hai Jag Mein Kapi,
Sankat Mochan Naam Tihaaro.||8||

|| Doha ||
Laal Deh Laali Lase, Aru Dhari Laal Langoor.
Bajra Deh Daanav Dalan, Jai Jai Jai Kapi Soor.
hindiMeaning: हनुमान अष्टक में हनुमान जी के आठ महान पराक्रमों का वर्णन है। बाल्यकाल में सूर्य को निगलना, सुग्रीव की सहायता, सीता माता की खोज, लंका दहन, संजीवनी बूटी लाकर लक्ष्मण के प्राण बचाना, नागपाश से मुक्ति, और अहिरावण का वध - ये सभी प्रसंग वर्णित हैं। प्रत्येक छंद के अंत में कहा गया है कि जगत में कौन नहीं जानता कि आपका नाम संकट मोचन है।
benefits: Classical tradition invokes this for removal of types of troubles and hardships
Invokes divine protection from negative forces
Invokes the blessing of courage and fearlessness
Traditionally recited for fulfillment of righteous desires
Associated in tradition with chronic and difficult diseases
Traditionally associated with victory in difficult situations
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Hanuman, Hanuman Ashtak (हनुमान अष्टक) stands as a sacred prayer. This revered text originates from Traditional - Tulsidas. Devotees recite this prayer to remove all types of troubles and hardships and grant divine protection from negative forces. It is also said to cure chronic and difficult diseases. For best results, devotees chant this on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Saturday (Shanivar) and during Hanuman Jayanti. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 12 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Hanuman Ashtak in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Hanuman Ashtak in Hindi with English transliteration. Eight verses praising Lord Hanuman's heroic deeds, the great remover of obstacles.
alsoKnownAs: Bal Samay Ravi Bhakshi Liyo Tab

seoFaqs.question: What is the Hanuman Ashtak and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Hanuman Ashtak (हनुमान अष्टक) — also called the Sankat Mochan Hanuman Ashtak — is one of the most beloved Awadhi devotional compositions of Goswami Tulsidas (1532-1623 CE), the saint-poet of the Hanuman Chalisa and Ramcharitmanas. It opens with the famous line 'Bal samay ravi bhakshi liyo tab, teenahun lok bhayo andhiyaro' (When in childhood you swallowed the Sun and all three worlds were plunged in darkness…). The composition is structured as eight savaiyya verses, each recounting one of Hanuman's eight greatest sankat-mochak (crisis-removing) exploits, and each ending with the immortal refrain 'Ko nahin jaanat hai jag mein kapi, Sankat Mochan naam tiharo' (Who in the world does not know, O Kapi, that your name is Sankat Mochan — Remover of Crises?). The famous Sankat Mochan Hanuman Mandir at Varanasi was established by Tulsidas himself. (Sources: Tulsidas tradition; Hanuman Ashtak text; Hindupedia.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the eight crises (sankat) recounted in the Hanuman Ashtak?
seoFaqs.answer: Each of the eight verses recounts one mahasankat that Hanuman resolved through his unique strength: (1) Verse 1 — as a child, Hanuman swallowed the rising Sun believing it a fruit, plunging the three worlds into darkness, and released it only on the prayers of the devas; (2) Verse 2 — saving Sugriva from Bali's terror at Rishyamukha hill and the muni's curse; (3) Verse 3 — finding Sita across the ocean when Angada and the entire vanara army had failed; (4) Verse 4 — burning Lanka and consoling Sita in the Ashoka Vatika; (5) Verse 5 — protecting Sita from Ravana's threats by slaying the rakshasas guarding her; (6) Verse 6 — bringing the Sanjeevani mountain to revive Lakshmana when struck by Indrajit's arrow; (7) Verse 7 — rescuing Rama, Lakshmana and the entire vanara army from the nagapasha by summoning Garuda; (8) Verse 8 — descending to Patala to slay Ahiravana and rescue Rama and Lakshmana from his sacrificial altar. (Sources: Tulsidas Hanuman Ashtak; Ramcharitmanas; Adbhuta Ramayana.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the refrain 'Sankat Mochan naam tiharo'?
seoFaqs.answer: The refrain 'Ko nahin jaanat hai jag mein kapi, Sankat Mochan naam tiharo' translates as 'Who in the world does not know, O Kapi (monkey-lord), that your very name is Sankat Mochan — the Remover of Crises?' This refrain is the theological heart of the entire Ashtak — Tulsidas declares that Hanuman's identity, his true name (naam), is itself Sankat Mochan: the very act and being of removing crises. Every recounted exploit demonstrates this name in action. The repetition across seven of the eight verses creates a liturgical mantra-like effect — by chanting the Ashtak, the devotee continually invokes Hanuman's identity-as-rescuer. The Sankat Mochan Mandir at Varanasi, founded by Tulsidas after his own vision of Hanuman in this form, takes its name from this very refrain. (Sources: Tulsidas Hanuman Ashtak; Sankat Mochan Mandir tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the Ahiravana episode in the eighth verse of the Hanuman Ashtak?
seoFaqs.answer: The eighth verse describes Hanuman's descent to Patala-loka to defeat Ahiravana — an episode preserved in the Adbhuta Ramayana, Ananda Ramayana, and several Puranic Ramayana traditions but not in the Valmiki Ramayana mainstream. Ahiravana, the brother (some traditions: cousin) of Ravana and king of Patala, abducted Rama and Lakshmana while they slept, intending to sacrifice them to Goddess Mahamaya at his altar. Hanuman descended to Patala, encountered his own son Makaradhwaja (born to a fish from his sweat after the Lanka-burning), defeated him, and entered Ahiravana's palace. Following Vibhishana's counsel, he simultaneously extinguished five lamps placed in the five directions (which held Ahiravana's life) and beheaded the demon. He then carried Rama on one shoulder and Lakshmana on the other back to the surface. (Sources: Adbhuta Ramayana; Ananda Ramayana; Tulsidas Hanuman Ashtak verse 8.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the closing doha 'Lal deh laali lase' about?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing doha of the Hanuman Ashtak — 'Laal deh laali lase, aru dhari laal langoor; Bajra deh daanav dalan, jai jai jai kapi soor' — is a beautiful pictorial description of Hanuman's iconic darshan: (1) 'Laal deh' — his complete body shines red/sindoor-coated; (2) 'Laali lase' — the rosy radiance glows from him; (3) 'Aru dhari laal langoor' — and he wears (carries) a long red tail; (4) 'Bajra deh' — his body is hard as vajra (diamond/thunderbolt); (5) 'Daanav dalan' — the crusher of demons; (6) 'Jai jai jai kapi soor' — victory, victory, victory to the heroic Kapi! The verse establishes the iconographic basis for the universal practice of offering sindoor (red vermilion) and chola (red garment) to Hanuman murtis on Tuesdays and Saturdays — a tradition originating in the Sundara Kanda episode where Hanuman saw Sita applying sindoor for Rama's longevity. (Sources: Tulsidas Hanuman Ashtak; Hanuman bhakti tradition; Ramcharitmanas Sundara Kanda.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits are attributed to regular recitation of the Hanuman Ashtak?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following phala to sincere Hanuman Ashtak path: (1) sankat-nivarana — removal of every type of trouble, crisis and hardship by invoking the very 'Sankat Mochan' name; (2) protection from negative forces, evil spirits, black magic and untimely death; (3) cure of chronic and difficult diseases — especially those resistant to medical treatment; (4) victory in difficult court cases, business disputes, and seemingly impossible situations; (5) courage and fearlessness in adversity — Hanuman's vajra-deha is invoked into the devotee; (6) fulfillment of righteous desires (dharma-anuguna kamana); (7) success in tasks where ordinary effort has failed (the central theme of all eight verses); (8) deepening of Rama-bhakti through Hanuman as Ram-doot. The practice is especially recommended on Tuesday and Saturday, with 11, 21 or 108 paths during a sankat-period. (Sources: Tulsidas tradition; Sankat Mochan Mandir bhakti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: How is the Hanuman Ashtak different from the Hanuman Chalisa?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are Tulsidas compositions but serve distinct purposes: (1) Hanuman Chalisa — 40 chaupais + 2 dohas covering Hanuman's complete biography from birth to enthronement, recited daily as an all-purpose stuti (praise-prayer) for general blessings, devotion and protection — gentle in mood and suitable for all occasions; (2) Hanuman Ashtak — 8 savaiyya verses + 1 doha focused specifically on eight crisis-resolution exploits, recited as a sankat-nivarana prayer when the devotee or family is facing specific trouble, illness or obstacle — more focused and fervent in mood, calling on Hanuman's identity as Sankat Mochan; (3) the Chalisa is in chaupai-doha meter (lighter Awadhi devotional rhythm); the Ashtak is in savaiyya meter (denser, more dramatic narrative meter). Many devotees recite the Chalisa daily and add the Ashtak during specific sankat-periods or sankat-nivarana anushthanas. (Sources: Tulsidas tradition; classical Awadhi prosody.)
